title: Charmed Life — 2 (Fragment 4)
sourceText:
Cat was so busy thinking of all this that, though he noticed a four-wheel cab standing in the road as he turned the corner by the Willing Warlock’s house, he paid no attention to it. He went to the side door and burst into the kitchen with his hatful of apples, shouting, “I say! Look what I’ve got, Mrs. Sharp!”

Mrs. Sharp was not there. Instead, standing in the middle of the kitchen, was a tall and quite extraordinarily well-dressed man.

Cat stared at him in some dismay. He was clearly a rich new Town Councillor. Nobody but those kind of people wore trousers with such pearly stripes, or coats of such beautiful velvet, or carried tall hats as shiny as their boots. The man’s hair was dark. It was smooth as his hat. Cat had no doubt that this was Gwendolen’s Dark Stranger, come to help her start ruling the world. And he should not have been in the kitchen at all. Visitors were always taken straight to the parlor.

“Oh, how do you do, sir. Will you come this way, sir?” he gasped.

The Dark Stranger gave him a wondering look. And well he might, Cat thought, looking around distractedly. The kitchen was in its usual mess. The range was all ash. On the table, Cat saw, to his further dismay, Mrs. Sharp had been making gingerbread men. The ingredients for the spell lay on one end of the table—all grubby newspaper packets and seedy little jars—and the gingerbread itself was strewn over the middle of the table. At the far end, the flies were gathering around the meat for lunch, which looked nearly as messy as the spell.

“Who are you?” said the Dark Stranger. “I have a feeling I should know you. What have you got in your hat?”

Cat was too busy staring around to attend properly, but he caught the last question. His pleasure returned. “Apples,” he said, showing the Stranger. “Lovely sweet ones. I’ve been scrumping.”

The Stranger looked grave. “Scrumping,” he said, “is a form of stealing.”

Cat knew that as well as he did. He thought it was very joyless, even for a Town Councillor, to point it out. “I know. But I bet you did it when you were my age.”

The Stranger coughed slightly and changed the subject. “You haven’t said yet who you are.”

“Sorry. Didn’t I?” said Cat. “I’m Eric Chant—only they always call me Cat.”

“Then is Gwendolen Chant your sister?” the Stranger asked. He was looking more and more austere and pitying. Cat suspected that he thought Mrs. Sharp’s kitchen was a den of vice.

“That’s right. Won’t you come this way?” Cat said, hoping to get the Stranger out of it. “It’s neater through here.”

“I had a letter from your sister,” the Stranger said, standing where he was. “She gave me the impression you had drowned with your parents.”

“You must have made a mistake,” Cat said distractedly. “I didn’t drown because I was holding on to Gwendolen, and she’s a witch. It’s cleaner through here.”

words.definition: In a very unusual or remarkable way.
words.example: He was extraordinarily well-dressed for the occasion.
words.exampleText: He was clearly a rich new Town Councillor. Nobody but those kind of people wore trousers with such pearly stripes, or coats of such beautiful velvet.

words.definition: A person who holds and uses something, especially power or a weapon.
words.example: A wielder of great influence in the council.
words.exampleText: Gwendolen’s Dark Stranger, come to help her start ruling the world.

words.definition: Very surprised or impressed by something unexpected.
words.example: I was astonished at how quickly she learned.
words.exampleText: Cat stared at him in some dismay.

words.definition: Having a lustrous, iridescent, or pearly quality or appearance.
words.example: She wore a pearlescent gown that shimmered in the light.
words.exampleText: ...trousers with such pearly stripes...

words.definition: A type of soft, thick cloth with a smooth surface.
words.example: He wore a velvet jacket to the party.
words.exampleText: ...coats of such beautiful velvet...

words.definition: A feeling of unhappiness and disappointment.
words.example: She looked at the test results with dismay.
words.exampleText: Cat stared at him in some dismay.

translations.ru: тайный сбор яблок, воровство фруктов с чужих деревьев

words.definition: The act of stealing fruit from someone's orchard or garden.
words.example: He was caught scrumping apples from the neighbour’s tree.
words.exampleText: I’ve been scrumping.

words.definition: To steal fruit from trees or gardens not belonging to one.
words.example: They scrumped cherries from the abandoned orchard.
words.exampleText: I’ve been scrumping.

words.definition: Severe or strict in manner, attitude, or appearance.
words.example: The monk lived an austere life.
words.exampleText: He was looking more and more austere and pitying.

words.definition: Feeling or showing sorrow and compassion for the sufferings or misfortunes of others.
words.example: She gave him a pitying glance when he fell.
words.exampleText: He was looking more and more austere and pitying.

words.definition: Immoral or wicked behaviour.
words.example: The city was riddled with vice.
words.exampleText: Cat suspected that he thought Mrs. Sharp’s kitchen was a den of vice.

words.definition: A wild animal's lair or resting place; metaphorically, a place where undesirable activities occur.
words.example: The den of the tiger was hidden in the jungle.
words.exampleText: Mrs. Sharp’s kitchen was a den of vice.

words.definition: Components or substances that make up a mixture or recipe.
words.example: The cake’s ingredients include flour and eggs.
words.exampleText: The ingredients for the spell lay on one end of the table.

words.definition: To throw or spread things over an area in an irregular manner.
words.example: She scattered seeds in the garden.
words.exampleText: ...the gingerbread itself was strewn over the middle of the table.

words.definition: Spread untidily over a surface or area.
words.example: The floor was strewn with papers after the wind blew the window open.
words.exampleText: The gingerbread itself was strewn over the middle of the table.

words.definition: Serious or solemn in appearance or manner.
words.example: He wore a grave expression after hearing the news.
words.exampleText: The Stranger looked grave.

words.definition: An elected member of a council.
words.example: The councillor addressed the town meeting.
words.exampleText: He was clearly a rich new Town Councillor.

words.definition: Reflecting light, bright and smooth in appearance.
words.example: Her shoes were shiny after polishing.
words.exampleText: ...tall hats as shiny as their boots.

words.definition: To die by submersion in and inhalation of water.
words.example: He nearly drowned while swimming in the lake.
words.exampleText: She gave me the impression you had drowned with your parents.

words.definition: Full of tears or inclined to shed tears.
words.example: She was tearful after the sad news.
words.exampleText: (Implied emotional tension but no direct example.)

words.definition: Untidy or dirty.
words.example: His desk was messy and cluttered.
words.exampleText: The kitchen was in its usual mess.

words.definition: Unable to concentrate because one is preoccupied or worried.
words.example: She seemed distracted during the meeting.
words.exampleText: “You must have made a mistake,” Cat said distractedly.

words.definition: An idea, feeling, or opinion about something or someone, often formed without conscious thought.
words.example: She made a good impression on her boss.
words.exampleText: She gave me the impression you had drowned with your parents.

words.definition: Notice taken of someone or something; the regarding of someone or something as interesting or important.
words.example: Pay attention to the teacher's instructions.
words.exampleText: Cat was too busy staring around to attend properly.

words.definition: To begin to govern or dominate a place or group.
words.example: They started ruling the club after the election.
words.exampleText: Gwendolen’s Dark Stranger, come to help her start ruling the world.

words.definition: To hear and understand the final question asked.
words.example: Though distracted, she caught the last question her friend asked.
words.exampleText: Cat was too busy staring around but he caught the last question.
